content.text: The Consent Framework

Microsoft Entra ID's consent framework controls which permissions applications can exercise on behalf of users or as themselves. Every time an application requests access to a resource (Microsoft Graph, your own API, or a third-party service), the platform checks whether a consent grant exists. If not, it prompts the user or blocks the request pending admin approval.

Consent is stored as an OAuth2PermissionGrant object in the tenant. Each grant records the client application, the resource application, the consenting principal (a specific user or 'AllPrincipals' for admin consent), and the list of scopes granted. These grants are evaluated at token issuance time, not at runtime when the API is called.

attrs.alt: Consent framework flow: request, decision, grant types

content.text: Permission Types

Entra ID distinguishes two axes of permissions. The first axis is delegated vs application: delegated permissions act on behalf of a signed-in user (the token carries both app and user identity), while application permissions act as the application itself with no user context. The second axis is the sensitivity of the scope, which determines whether a user can self-consent or whether admin consent is required.
Dimension
Delegated permission
Application permission
Token type
Access token with user + app claims
Access token with app claims only (no user)
Who consents
User (for low-risk scopes) or admin
Admin only — always requires admin consent
Effective permissions
Intersection of app's permissions and user's own rights
Full scope of the permission, no user restriction
Typical use
Web apps, SPAs, mobile apps with sign-in
Background services, daemons, scheduled jobs
Example scope
Mail.Read (user's mailbox only)
Mail.Read (any mailbox in tenant)

Application permissions are high-privilege by design. A daemon with Mail.Read can read every mailbox in the tenant. Always scope application permissions to the minimum required and enforce Conditional Access policies on service principals.
Scope Naming and the .default Scope

OAuth 2.0 scopes in Entra ID follow the pattern resource-uri/scope-name, for example https://graph.microsoft.com/Mail.Read or api://myapp/data.read. When you request the .default scope for a resource (https://graph.microsoft.com/.default), Entra ID issues a token containing all permissions that have been pre-consented for that resource — both delegated and application permissions that the app already holds a grant for.

# Python example: requesting specific scopes vs .default
from msal import ConfidentialClientApplication

app = ConfidentialClientApplication(
    client_id='<client-id>',
    client_credential='<client-secret>',
    authority='https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>'
)

# Client Credentials flow: always use .default
# The scopes you can get are those already admin-consented on the service principal
result = app.acquire_token_for_client(
    scopes=['https://graph.microsoft.com/.default']
)
# Token will contain all application permissions granted to this app

# Auth Code flow: request only what you need for this operation
# Incremental consent: you can request scopes across multiple sign-ins
auth_url, state = app.initiate_auth_code_flow(
    scopes=['User.Read', 'Mail.Read'],   # request specific scopes
    redirect_uri='https://app.example.com/callback'
)
# On a second operation, request additional scope
auth_url_2, state_2 = app.initiate_auth_code_flow(
    scopes=['User.Read', 'Mail.Read', 'Calendars.ReadWrite'],  # incremental
    redirect_uri='https://app.example.com/callback'
)
# Entra prompts for Calendars.ReadWrite only if not already granted
User Consent Policies

Tenant administrators control which scopes users can self-consent to via the User Consent Settings policy. Three configurations are available: allow user consent for all apps, allow user consent for apps from verified publishers only, or block all user consent (admin consent required for everything). The recommended baseline is 'verified publishers, selected permissions'.

# Read current user consent policy
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.Read.All', 'Policy.ReadWrite.Authorization'

$policy = Get-MgPolicyAuthorizationPolicy
$policy.PermissionGrantPolicyIdsAssignedToDefaultUserRole
# Typical values:
#   ManagePermissionGrantsForSelf.microsoft-user-default-legacy-v2  (all apps)
#   ManagePermissionGrantsForSelf.microsoft-user-default-low        (verified + low risk)
#   (empty array)                                                    (block user consent)

# Restrict to verified publishers with low-risk permissions only
Update-MgPolicyAuthorizationPolicy -PermissionGrantPolicyIdsAssignedToDefaultUserRole `
    @('ManagePermissionGrantsForSelf.microsoft-user-default-low')

# Block all user consent (require admin for everything)
Update-MgPolicyAuthorizationPolicy -PermissionGrantPolicyIdsAssignedToDefaultUserRole @()

# Create a custom permission grant policy (allow specific permissions only)
$customPolicy = New-MgPolicyPermissionGrantPolicy `
    -Id 'custom-low-risk-policy' `
    -DisplayName 'Allow openid profile email only' `
    -Description 'Users may consent to basic sign-in scopes'

# Add an include condition: openid, profile, email from any publisher
New-MgPolicyPermissionGrantPolicyInclude -PermissionGrantPolicyId $customPolicy.Id `
    -PermissionClassification 'low' `
    -PermissionType 'delegated' `
    -ClientApplicationsFromVerifiedPublisherOnly $false

# Apply the custom policy
Update-MgPolicyAuthorizationPolicy `
    -PermissionGrantPolicyIdsAssignedToDefaultUserRole @('ManagePermissionGrantsForSelf.custom-low-risk-policy')
Admin Consent

Admin consent grants a permission to all users in the tenant simultaneously. It creates an OAuth2PermissionGrant with consentType='AllPrincipals' for delegated permissions, or an AppRoleAssignment for application permissions. Admins can grant consent via the Entra portal, via the /adminconsent endpoint, or programmatically.

# Grant admin consent for delegated permissions (on behalf of all users)
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'DelegatedPermissionGrant.ReadWrite.All'

$clientSpId = '<enterprise-app-service-principal-id>'
$graphSpId  = (Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'").Id

# Check if a grant already exists
$existing = Get-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -Filter "clientId eq '$clientSpId' and resourceId eq '$graphSpId'"

if ($existing) {
    # Update existing grant to add more scopes
    $currentScopes = $existing.Scope -split ' '
    $newScopes = ($currentScopes + @('Mail.Read', 'User.ReadBasic.All') | Select-Object -Unique) -join ' '
    Update-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -OAuth2PermissionGrantId $existing.Id -Scope $newScopes
} else {
    # Create a new tenant-wide grant
    New-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -BodyParameter @{
        clientId    = $clientSpId
        consentType = 'AllPrincipals'
        resourceId  = $graphSpId
        scope       = 'User.Read Mail.Read User.ReadBasic.All'
    }
}

# Grant application permissions (AppRoleAssignment)
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All'
$appRoleId = ($graphSpId | Get-MgServicePrincipal).AppRoles |
    Where-Object { $_.Value -eq 'Mail.Read' } | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Id

New-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment `
    -ServicePrincipalId $clientSpId `
    -PrincipalId $clientSpId `
    -ResourceId $graphSpId `
    -AppRoleId $appRoleId

# Generate an admin consent URL programmatically
# Send this URL to a tenant admin to consent to your multi-tenant app
import urllib.parse

CLIENT_ID   = '<your-app-client-id>'
REDIRECT    = 'https://app.example.com/auth/admin-consent/callback'
STATE       = 'random-csrf-token'

# Tenant-specific admin consent (admin of a specific tenant)
params = {
    'client_id':     CLIENT_ID,
    'redirect_uri':  REDIRECT,
    'state':         STATE,
    # scope=/.default includes all configured API permissions
    'scope':         'https://graph.microsoft.com/.default',
}
consent_url = ('https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/v2.0/adminconsent?'
               + urllib.parse.urlencode(params))

# Common endpoint (admin can consent on behalf of their own tenant)
consent_url_common = ('https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/v2.0/adminconsent?'
                      + urllib.parse.urlencode(params))

# After consent, the redirect includes:
#   ?admin_consent=True&tenant=<tenant-id>&state=<state>
# Verify state matches, then create the OAuth2PermissionGrant record
Pre-Authorization and Seamless Consent

When a first-party client calls your API, you can pre-authorize it in the API's App Registration. Pre-authorized clients receive tokens for the specified scopes without any consent prompt — the API owner is asserting that it trusts these specific clients. This is how Microsoft Teams tabs or Office Add-ins call backend APIs without triggering consent dialogs.

# Pre-authorize a client application to call your API without consent prompts
# This is set on the API's App Registration, not the client's

$apiAppId = '<your-api-app-registration-id>'  # the API being called
$clientAppId = '<first-party-client-app-id>'  # the pre-authorized caller

# First, get the scope IDs defined on the API
$apiApp = Get-MgApplication -ApplicationId $apiAppId
$readScopeId = ($apiApp.Api.Oauth2PermissionScopes | Where-Object Value -eq 'data.read').Id

# Add the pre-authorized application
$currentPreAuth = $apiApp.Api.PreAuthorizedApplications ?? @()
$newPreAuth = $currentPreAuth + @{
    AppId = $clientAppId
    DelegatedPermissionIds = @($readScopeId.ToString())
}

Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId $apiAppId -Api @{
    PreAuthorizedApplications = $newPreAuth
}

# Verify
(Get-MgApplication -ApplicationId $apiAppId).Api.PreAuthorizedApplications |
    Select-Object AppId, DelegatedPermissionIds

Pre-authorization means the API trusts the client unconditionally. Only pre-authorize applications you control or strongly trust. A compromised pre-authorized client can call your API on behalf of any user without triggering additional consent.
Admin Consent Workflow (Entra ID P2)

When user consent is blocked and a user tries to access an app that lacks admin consent, Entra ID can redirect them to an admin consent workflow instead of showing an error. The user submits a request with a business justification; admins receive email notification and can approve or deny from the Entra portal. This decouples app onboarding from helpdesk tickets.

# Enable and configure the admin consent workflow
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.ReadWrite.ConditionalAccess'

# Enable the workflow and designate reviewers
$adminId1 = (Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq 'itadmin@contoso.com'").Id
$adminId2 = (Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq 'secadmin@contoso.com'").Id

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PUT `
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/adminConsentRequestPolicy' `
    -Body (@{
        isEnabled = $true
        version   = 1
        notifyReviewers    = $true
        remindersEnabled   = $true
        requestDurationInDays = 30
        reviewers = @(
            @{ query = "/users/$adminId1"; queryType = 'MicrosoftGraph' },
            @{ query = "/users/$adminId2"; queryType = 'MicrosoftGraph' }
        )
    } | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10)

# List pending admin consent requests
Get-MgIdentityGovernanceAppConsentRequest -Filter "pendingScopes/any()" |
    Select-Object Id, AppDisplayName, CreatedDateTime,
        @{N='RequestedBy';E={$_.UserConsentRequests[0]?.CreatedBy?.User?.UserPrincipalName}},
        @{N='Justification';E={$_.UserConsentRequests[0]?.Reason}}

# Approve a pending request
$requestId = '<admin-consent-request-id>'
$userConsentId = '<user-consent-request-id>'
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/appConsent/appConsentRequests/$requestId/userConsentRequests/$userConsentId/approve" `
    -Body (@{ justification = 'Verified business need for HR system integration' } | ConvertTo-Json)
Auditing and Revoking Consent

Over time, tenants accumulate consent grants for abandoned apps, overly broad scopes, or apps from unverified publishers. A regular consent audit identifies grants that should be revoked and apps that should be reviewed for removal.

# Audit all OAuth2 permission grants in the tenant
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'DelegatedPermissionGrant.ReadWrite.All','Application.Read.All'

# Get all delegated permission grants with app names resolved
$grants = Get-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -All
$report = $grants | ForEach-Object {
    $g = $_
    $clientName   = (Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $g.ClientId -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)?.DisplayName
    $resourceName = (Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $g.ResourceId -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)?.DisplayName
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        Client      = $clientName ?? $g.ClientId
        Resource    = $resourceName ?? $g.ResourceId
        ConsentType = $g.ConsentType    # AllPrincipals or Principal
        Scopes      = $g.Scope
        ExpiryTime  = $g.ExpiryTime
        GrantId     = $g.Id
    }
}

# Flag broad grants: any app with more than 5 scopes
$report | Where-Object { ($_.Scopes -split ' ').Count -gt 5 } | Format-Table

# Flag non-expiring tenant-wide grants (ExpiryTime is null = never expires)
$report | Where-Object { $_.ConsentType -eq 'AllPrincipals' -and -not $_.ExpiryTime } |
    Select-Object Client, Resource, Scopes | Format-Table

# Revoke a specific grant
Remove-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -OAuth2PermissionGrantId '<grant-id>'

# Revoke all grants for a specific third-party app (before removing it)
$thirdPartySpId = '<third-party-service-principal-id>'
Get-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -Filter "clientId eq '$thirdPartySpId'" |
    ForEach-Object { Remove-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -OAuth2PermissionGrantId $_.Id }
Get-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment -ServicePrincipalId $thirdPartySpId |
    ForEach-Object {
        Remove-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment `
            -ServicePrincipalId $thirdPartySpId `
            -AppRoleAssignmentId $_.Id
    }
Audit signal
Risk
Remediation
App from unverified publisher with broad scopes
Unknown third-party accessing tenant data
Revoke grants, remove service principal
AllPrincipals grant with no expiry
Permanent tenant-wide data access
Re-grant with expiry or convert to per-user
Inactive app (no sign-in in 90 days) with grants
Abandoned app still authorized
Remove app and revoke all grants
Application permission to Mail.ReadWrite
Can read/write all mailboxes
Verify need; enforce CA policy on the SP
User-consented scope beyond policy baseline
Policy change left orphaned user grants
Run consent revocation sweep for the scope
Permission Classification

Entra ID classifies delegated permissions by risk level to help enforce least-privilege user consent. Microsoft-defined permissions are classified as 'low', 'medium', 'high', or unclassified. The user consent policy can allow self-consent for 'low' classified permissions only, preventing users from accidentally granting high-risk permissions to third-party apps.

# List permission classifications for Microsoft Graph
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.Read.All'

$graphSpId = (Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'").Id

# Get all permission classifications
Get-MgServicePrincipalDelegatedPermissionClassification -ServicePrincipalId $graphSpId |
    Select-Object PermissionName, Classification |
    Sort-Object Classification, PermissionName

# Typical low-risk classifications:
# openid, profile, email, offline_access, User.Read

# Add a custom classification for your own API's scope
$myApiSpId = (Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "displayName eq 'My API'").Id
$scopeId   = (Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $myApiSpId).Oauth2PermissionScopes |
    Where-Object Value -eq 'data.read' | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Id

New-MgServicePrincipalDelegatedPermissionClassification `
    -ServicePrincipalId $myApiSpId `
    -PermissionId $scopeId.ToString() `
    -PermissionName 'data.read' `
    -Classification 'low'   # allows user self-consent for this scope

# List unclassified scopes (neither low/medium/high) on Graph
$allScopes = (Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $graphSpId).Oauth2PermissionScopes
$classified = Get-MgServicePrincipalDelegatedPermissionClassification -ServicePrincipalId $graphSpId
$classifiedNames = $classified | Select-Object -ExpandProperty PermissionName
$allScopes | Where-Object { $_.Value -notin $classifiedNames } |
    Select-Object Value, AdminConsentDisplayName |
    Sort-Object Value
Classification
What it means
User consent allowed?
low
Minimal data exposure (basic profile, openid)
Yes, if policy permits low-risk consent
medium
Some sensitive data (e.g. read files)
Tenant-admin decision via custom policy
high
Wide data access (all mailboxes, full read/write)
No — admin consent always required
(unclassified)
Not yet assessed by Microsoft
Follows policy; typically blocked by safe defaults
Incremental and Dynamic Consent

Rather than requesting all permissions upfront, modern applications use incremental consent: request only the scopes needed for the current operation, then request additional scopes when the user accesses a feature that needs them. This reduces friction at first sign-in and gives users a clearer picture of what each permission enables.

Never request permissions 'just in case' — users see all requested permissions and can reject or revoke them. Request the minimum scope for each operation. For delegated flows, always try acquire_token_silent first; only redirect to /authorize when the cache misses or consent is required.
Consent in Multi-Tenant Applications

Multi-tenant applications registered with signInAudience='AzureADMultipleOrgs' can be accessed by users from any Entra ID tenant. Each tenant must independently consent to the application's permissions. The first user or admin from a tenant who accesses the app triggers consent, which creates a service principal in that tenant.

# Validate that admin consent was actually granted after the /adminconsent redirect
# Callback handler for admin consent flow
import requests
from flask import request as flask_request, jsonify

@flask_app.route('/auth/admin-consent/callback')
def admin_consent_callback():
    # Entra redirects to: /callback?admin_consent=True&tenant=<tid>&state=<state>
    admin_consent = flask_request.args.get('admin_consent')
    tenant_id     = flask_request.args.get('tenant')
    state         = flask_request.args.get('state')
    error         = flask_request.args.get('error')
    error_desc    = flask_request.args.get('error_description', '')

    if error:
        # Common: access_denied if admin clicked Cancel
        return jsonify({'error': error, 'description': error_desc}), 400

    if admin_consent != 'True':
        return jsonify({'error': 'consent_not_granted'}), 400

    # Verify state matches what we set (CSRF protection)
    stored_state = session.pop('consent_state', None)
    if state != stored_state:
        return jsonify({'error': 'state_mismatch'}), 400

    # Record that this tenant has consented (store in your database)
    save_tenant_consent(tenant_id=tenant_id, consented_at='now')

    # Verify by checking the service principal exists in the tenant
    # Use app-only token (Client Credentials) for the app's home tenant
    token_resp = requests.post(
        f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant_id}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
        data={
            'client_id':     '<client-id>',
            'client_secret': '<client-secret>',
            'scope':         'https://graph.microsoft.com/.default',
            'grant_type':    'client_credentials',
        }
    )
    token = token_resp.json().get('access_token')
    if not token:
        return jsonify({'error': 'could not get token after consent'}), 500

    return jsonify({'status': 'consent_recorded', 'tenant': tenant_id})

def save_tenant_consent(tenant_id, consented_at):
    # Placeholder: persist to your database
    print(f'Tenant {tenant_id} consented at {consented_at}')
Scenario
Consent behavior
First user from new tenant accesses app
Consent prompt shown; grant stored in their tenant on approval
Admin uses /adminconsent endpoint
Tenant-wide grant created; no per-user prompt needed afterwards
App requests new scope
Existing users prompted for the new scope on next sign-in (incremental consent)
Admin revokes grant
Existing tokens remain valid until expiry; refresh tokens fail from revocation time
Tenant removes the service principal
All users in that tenant lose access; re-consent recreates the SP
Monitoring Consent Events in Audit Logs

Every consent grant and revocation is recorded in the Entra audit log under the 'Application Management' category. Monitoring these events detects shadow IT (users consenting to unknown third-party apps) and unauthorized permission escalations.

# Query audit logs for consent events in the last 30 days
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AuditLog.Read.All'

$since = (Get-Date).AddDays(-30).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')

# Consent grants by users
Get-MgAuditLogDirectoryAudit `
    -Filter "activityDisplayName eq 'Consent to application' and activityDateTime ge $since" `
    -Top 100 |
    ForEach-Object {
        $entry = $_
        $appName = ($entry.TargetResources | Where-Object Type -eq 'ServicePrincipal' | Select-Object -First 1).DisplayName
        $scopes  = ($entry.AdditionalDetails | Where-Object Key -eq 'ConsentContext.Scopes').Value
        [PSCustomObject]@{
            Time        = $entry.ActivityDateTime
            InitiatedBy = $entry.InitiatedBy.User?.UserPrincipalName
            App         = $appName
            Scopes      = $scopes
            IsAdminConsent = ($entry.AdditionalDetails | Where-Object Key -eq 'ConsentContext.IsAdminConsent').Value
        }
    } | Sort-Object Time -Descending | Format-Table

# Alert on admin consent for high-privilege scopes
Get-MgAuditLogDirectoryAudit `
    -Filter "activityDisplayName eq 'Consent to application' and activityDateTime ge $since" `
    -Top 500 |
    Where-Object {
        $scopes = ($_.AdditionalDetails | Where-Object Key -eq 'ConsentContext.Scopes').Value
        $highRisk = @('Mail.ReadWrite','Files.ReadWrite.All','Directory.ReadWrite.All','RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory')
        $highRisk | Where-Object { $scopes -match $_ }
    } |
    Select-Object ActivityDateTime,
        @{N='Admin';E={$_.InitiatedBy.User?.UserPrincipalName}},
        @{N='App';E={($_.TargetResources | Select-Object -First 1).DisplayName}},
        @{N='Scopes';E={($_.AdditionalDetails | Where-Object Key -eq 'ConsentContext.Scopes').Value}}
Summary

The consent framework is the enforcement layer between application permission requests and actual data access in Microsoft Entra ID. Understanding the delegated vs application permission distinction and the four consent mechanisms (user consent, admin consent, pre-authorization, and consent workflow) is essential for building secure and usable application integrations.
Delegated permissions act on behalf of a user; application permissions act as the app itself with no user context
The .default scope requests all pre-consented permissions — required for Client Credentials flow
User consent policy controls which scopes users can self-consent to; 'verified publisher + low risk' is the recommended baseline
Admin consent (AllPrincipals) covers all tenant users; application permissions always require admin consent
Pre-authorization in the API's App Registration eliminates consent prompts for trusted first-party clients
Admin consent workflow (P2) lets users request access with justification rather than receiving a hard error
Regularly audit OAuth2PermissionGrants for broad, non-expiring, or abandoned grants and revoke them
